title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a woman called Nia Evans, who volunteers with a sea rescue team.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today we're meeting Nia Evans, who volunteers with a sea rescue team. Nia, how did you join the team? || Guest: I grew up near the coast and learned to sail when I was young, but I never imagined doing rescue work. A friend took me to an open evening at the station. [20]After hearing the volunteers describe how they helped local families, I signed up for training straight away[/20]. || Interviewer: Was the training what you expected? || Guest: I thought it would mostly be about driving boats fast, and that part was exciting. But there was much more to learn. [21]The radio communication was hardest for me because every message has to be short, clear and correct[/21]. || Interviewer: What do you enjoy about being part of the team? || Guest: The rescue calls are important, of course, but we don't want people to be in danger. [22]I really enjoy visiting schools and teaching children how to stay safe near water[/22]. They remember more than adults sometimes. || Interviewer: Do people understand what volunteers do? || Guest: Not always. Some think we are paid full-time, but we all have normal jobs. [23]People are often surprised that training happens in the evenings after work and at weekends[/23]. It takes a lot of commitment. || Interviewer: Has any rescue changed the way you think? || Guest: Yes, one family got into trouble in a small boat on a sunny day. The sea looked calm, but the engine failed. [24]It reminded me that problems can happen even when conditions seem safe[/24], so preparation matters. || Interviewer: What advice would you give to people going out on the water? || Guest: Check the weather and tell someone where you're going. And please don't rely only on your phone. [25]Wearing a proper life jacket is the simple thing that can save you[/25]. || Interviewer: Before we finish, Nia, do rescue volunteers need to be especially brave? || Guest: People often use that word, but I think training is more important than bravery. If you know what to do, you can stay calm. We never want anyone taking silly risks to look heroic. The team works because everyone follows the same steps and trusts each other. That is much safer than one person trying to be brave alone. || Interviewer: How do you balance volunteering with the rest of your life? || Guest: With difficulty sometimes. My family understand, but I still miss meals or change plans when there is a call or a training session. I try to keep one evening a week completely free. The team leaders are good at reminding us that tired volunteers are not useful volunteers. Rest is part of being ready. || Interviewer: Has volunteering changed your view of the sea? || Guest: Yes. I still love it, maybe more than before, but I respect it more. A beach can look friendly from the shore and still be dangerous. I notice tides, wind and people's behaviour all the time now. Even on holiday I catch myself checking whether children are too far out. || Interviewer: Finally, what do you do when you need new ideas? || Guest: I try to step away from the main work for a short time. Going for a walk, visiting another place or talking to someone outside the job usually helps. If I sit at a desk worrying, nothing improves. I also keep a small notebook, because useful ideas often arrive at inconvenient moments, like on a bus or while making dinner.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today we're meeting Nia Evans, who volunteers with a sea rescue team. Nia, how did you join the team?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I grew up near the coast and learned to sail when I was young, but I never imagined doing rescue work. A friend took me to an open evening at the station. After hearing the volunteers describe how they helped local families, I signed up for training straight away.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Was the training what you expected?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I thought it would mostly be about driving boats fast, and that part was exciting. But there was much more to learn. The radio communication was hardest for me because every message has to be short, clear and correct.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you enjoy about being part of the team?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: The rescue calls are important, of course, but we don't want people to be in danger. I really enjoy visiting schools and teaching children how to stay safe near water. They remember more than adults sometimes.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do people understand what volunteers do?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Not always. Some think we are paid full-time, but we all have normal jobs. People are often surprised that training happens in the evenings after work and at weekends. It takes a lot of commitment.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Has any rescue changed the way you think?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, one family got into trouble in a small boat on a sunny day. The sea looked calm, but the engine failed. It reminded me that problems can happen even when conditions seem safe, so preparation matters.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What advice would you give to people going out on the water?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Check the weather and tell someone where you're going. And please don't rely only on your phone. Wearing a proper life jacket is the simple thing that can save you.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Before we finish, Nia, do rescue volunteers need to be especially brave?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: People often use that word, but I think training is more important than bravery. If you know what to do, you can stay calm. We never want anyone taking silly risks to look heroic. The team works because everyone follows the same steps and trusts each other. That is much safer than one person trying to be brave alone.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How do you balance volunteering with the rest of your life?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: With difficulty sometimes. My family understand, but I still miss meals or change plans when there is a call or a training session. I try to keep one evening a week completely free. The team leaders are good at reminding us that tired volunteers are not useful volunteers. Rest is part of being ready.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Has volunteering changed your view of the sea?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes. I still love it, maybe more than before, but I respect it more. A beach can look friendly from the shore and still be dangerous. I notice tides, wind and people's behaviour all the time now. Even on holiday I catch myself checking whether children are too far out.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Finally, what do you do when you need new ideas?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I try to step away from the main work for a short time. Going for a walk, visiting another place or talking to someone outside the job usually helps. If I sit at a desk worrying, nothing improves. I also keep a small notebook, because useful ideas often arrive at inconvenient moments, like on a bus or while making dinner.

questions.question: Why did Nia decide to join the rescue team?

options.A: She wanted to use her sailing skills.
options.B: She was encouraged by local volunteers.
options.C: She had always wanted to do rescue work.

questions.explanation: Nia says hearing volunteers describe helping local families made her sign up.

questions.question: What did Nia find most difficult in training?
questions.explanation: She says radio communication was hardest because messages must be short, clear and correct.

questions.question: What part of volunteering does Nia enjoy most?

options.B: going out on important rescue calls

questions.explanation: Nia says she really enjoys visiting schools and teaching children about water safety.

questions.question: What surprises people about the volunteers?

options.A: They need a lot of free time for training.
options.B: They are paid for some of their work.
options.C: They all have jobs connected with the sea.

questions.explanation: Nia says people are surprised that training happens after work and at weekends.

questions.question: What did one rescue teach Nia?

options.A: Small boats should not go far from land.
options.B: Danger can appear on a calm day.
options.C: Families often forget to check engines.

questions.explanation: She says the rescue reminded her that problems can happen even when conditions seem safe.

questions.question: What does Nia say is especially important for people on the water?
questions.explanation: Nia says wearing a proper life jacket can save you.
